title: SiLU
description: A smooth activation that multiplies each FFN hidden value by its sigmoid gate.
openingSummary: SiLU smoothly gates each FFN hidden value with its own sigmoid score, giving transformer MLPs a softer activation than ReLU and setting up the gate used in SwiGLU blocks.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: SiLU stands for sigmoid linear unit. For each hidden value x inside an FFN, it outputs x multiplied by sigmoid(x). Large positive values pass through strongly, values near zero are softened, and negative values are reduced smoothly instead of being cut off sharply.

whatItOptimizes.title: What It Optimizes
whatItOptimizes.body: SiLU optimizes for a smoother hidden activation rule. Instead of a hard cutoff, it lets the negative side fade gradually, which often fits modern wide transformer FFNs better than a sharp zero clamp.

practicalBenefit.title: Practical Benefit
practicalBenefit.body: SiLU matters because it shows up both as a plain FFN activation and as the gate nonlinearity inside SwiGLU. Learning this page makes the jump to gated FFN variants much easier.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: The token state expands into a wide hidden layer, then each hidden value is multiplied by its own sigmoid gate. Large positive values mostly survive, small values are softened, and negative values shrink rather than snapping to zero before projection back to model width.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: The formula below shows the smooth self-gating rule directly. The sigmoid term is what turns one hidden value into its own soft gate.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: Compared with ReLU and LeakyReLU, SiLU is the smooth option in this activation family. It still stays inside one dense FFN path, but it prepares the reader for the stronger two-branch gate used by SwiGLU.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: Many recent decoder-only language models and gated FFN blocks use SiLU or a close relative, especially when authors want a smoother dense baseline before moving to SwiGLU.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: SiLU is smoother than ReLU-family activations, but it is still a dense per-token activation, not a separate routing or expert mechanism. Every token still runs through the full shared FFN path.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: SiLU sits at an important bridge point between classic activation pages and modern gated FFN pages. Readers who understand SiLU can usually decode SwiGLU much faster.

computeFlow.alt: Activation comparison graph showing how ReLU, LeakyReLU, and SiLU reshape hidden values inside the same FFN path
computeFlow.caption: SiLU keeps the same dense FFN slot as ReLU-family activations, but it replaces the hard cutoff with a smooth self-gating curve

comparisonTable.caption: How SiLU compares with nearby activation variants

relu.negativeBranch: Clamp every negative hidden value to 0
relu.mainTradeoff: Simple and sparse, but fully drops negative signal

leakyRelu.negativeBranch: Keep a small slope alpha x for x < 0
leakyRelu.transitionShape: Hard corner with a weak negative path
leakyRelu.mainTradeoff: Keeps some negative gradient, but still uses a piecewise-linear cutoff

silu.negativeBranch: Shrink negative values smoothly with x sigma(x)
silu.mainTradeoff: Smoother feature filtering, but more complex than the ReLU family baseline

x.definition: One hidden value inside the expanded FFN state.

sigma.definition: Sigmoid gate applied to that same hidden value.

negativeBehavior.label: Negative hidden values shrink smoothly instead of snapping to 0
